description: Open 2C with a hand too strong for a one-level opening, then use waiting, positive-suit responses, and second-negative continuations to keep the auction organized.
purpose: Teach Larry-style strong 2C structure: responder waits with 2D unless holding 8+ HCP and a good 5+ suit, opener clarifies balanced versus natural shape, and the second-negative structure still lets the partnership stop below game when opener repeats the suit.
scopeNote: Larry's 2C opening with `2D` waiting as the near-universal response, plus first-round forcing rebids and the cheapest-new-suit-at-3-level 'second negative'. Balanced `2NT` / `3NT` rebid split is authored as `22-24` vs `25-27` (not pinned by the authority). Unbalanced strong-2C qualifier uses a `9+` playing-trick heuristic as a best-effort proxy for Larry's judgement-based rule. Opener stop-short continuations after second negative, natural continuation that cancels the second negative, post-positive / post-2NT extended continuation trees, the 2NT-positive branch (removed in b7981331), and competitive methods are all out of scope. Gerber after 2C openings is not modeled here.

snapshot.text:
# Two Club Opening

By: Larry Cohen

One of Larry's significant concerns is assigning a specific point range to a 2♣ opening. He deliberately avoids placing a range on the convention card.

## Point Requirements

For balanced hands, a clear range exists. Since 2NT shows 20-21 points, a 2♣ opening with a balanced hand requires 22 or more points, followed by a rebid of 2NT or 3NT.

For unbalanced hands, "the HCP range is a matter of judgement." High card strength matters less than hand composition. Larry illustrates this with examples:

He *would* open 2♣ with an 18-point hand containing ♠AKQ10876 ♥AQ32 ♦K2 ♣— because avoiding a 1♠-P-P-P result justifies the bid.

He *would not* open 2♣ with a 22-point hand of ♠AKJ2 ♥AKJ2 ♦2 ♣AQ32 because describing this hand after starting with 2♣ becomes problematic.

## Responses

Larry opposes step or control responses, preferring:

**2♦ = Waiting bid** (0+ HCP, any distribution), allowing opener room to further describe his hand.

Responder deviates from 2♦ only with substantial strength *and* a quality suit: 8+ HCP with a decent five-card suit containing two or more top honors.

## Continuations After 2♦

Opener's rebids (except in notrump) force for one round. Partnerships can stop below game only through a "2nd negative"—the cheapest new suit at the three level, which is artificial and permits responder to pass if opener repeats his suit.

Examples of partscore sequences:

2♣-2♦-2♥-3♣-3♥

2♣-2♦-3♣-3♦-4♣

These auctions occur "once a decade."

If responder does anything other than issue a 2nd negative, game is forced. When responder uses a 2nd negative then repeats a suit, it becomes natural, showing genuine length in that suit.

Responder raises of opener's rebids follow the principle of fast arrival. A jump raise like 2♣-2♦-2♥-4♥ shows weakness, while a slower raise like 2♣-2♦-2♥-3♥ shows greater strength.

## In Competition

Responder's double of an overcall shows a terrible hand (0-3 HCP). Pass by responder shows a game-forcing hand. Responder's free bid indicates 8+ HCP with a quality five-card suit. When opponents overcall and opener doubles, it is takeout.

teaching.tradeoff: Using 2C as a strong artificial opening means you lose the natural 2C bid, but the partnership gains a forcing structure for hands too strong to risk a quiet one-level opening.
teaching.principle: Start with the catch-all 2D waiting response unless responder has both values and real suit quality; after a second negative opener repeats the suit so responder can pass or later show that suit naturally, and once opener shows a balanced hand the usual notrump methods take over.

commonMistakes.text: Do not treat 2D as a pure 0-7 response.
commonMistakes.reason: Larry uses 2D as the default waiting call whenever responder lacks a good enough positive suit, so balanced 8- or 9-counts often start there too.

commonMistakes.text: Do not call a five-card suit positive without honor quality.
commonMistakes.reason: A positive response promises about 8+ HCP and a decent 5+ card suit, usually with two of the top three honors or three of the top five.

commonMistakes.text: Do not forget that 2C is game-forcing unless opener rebids 2NT after the waiting response.
commonMistakes.reason: The only quiet exception is 2C-2D-2NT, where responder may pass with a yarborough; every other line keeps the auction forcing.

commonMistakes.text: Do not judge unbalanced strong-2C hands by raw HCP alone.
commonMistakes.reason: Larry evaluates trick-taking power, not just points. Strong unbalanced hands belong in 2C when they are too powerful to risk a one-level opening.

definitions.description: 22+ HCP balanced or an unbalanced powerhouse with near-game tricks qualifies for the core strong 2C opening rule
2D is the catch-all waiting bid when responder lacks a Larry-style positive suit response
8+ HCP with a good 5+ suit (positive response)
Hearts suit quality passes Larry's positive-response standard: 5+ cards with 2 of top 3 honors (A/K/Q) or 3 of top 5 honors (A/K/Q/J/T)
Spades suit quality passes Larry's positive-response standard: 5+ cards with 2 of top 3 honors (A/K/Q) or 3 of top 5 honors (A/K/Q/J/T)
Clubs suit quality passes Larry's positive-response standard: 5+ cards with 2 of top 3 honors (A/K/Q) or 3 of top 5 honors (A/K/Q/J/T)
Diamonds suit quality passes Larry's positive-response standard: 5+ cards with 2 of top 3 honors (A/K/Q) or 3 of top 5 honors (A/K/Q/J/T)

summaryCard.trigger: You open strong two clubs, partner responds
summaryCard.partnership: Agree that two diamonds is the default waiting reply, that positive suit responses need real suit quality, and that after a second negative opener repeats the suit so responder may pass or later show that suit naturally. After opener rebids two notrump, Stayman and Jacoby Transfers come from the sibling no-trump modules rather than this fixture.

whenToUse.gloss: Use the artificial opening when partnership strength already commits you to a forcing auction.
Use the waiting reply whenever responder lacks a Larry-style positive suit response.
Show a real suit immediately only with eight-plus HCP and a good five-plus card suit.

workedAuctions.label: Waiting first, then the balanced clarification

calls.rationale: Opener starts with the artificial game force.
Responder waits because no suit-quality positive is available.
With a true bust, responder may now stop short of game.

workedAuctions.label: Positive suit response, then fast arrival

calls.rationale: Opener begins with the artificial force.
Responder shows 8+ HCP and a good 5+ heart suit.
Opener raises the suit, showing real support.
Fast arrival makes the double raise a weaker signoff than the single raise.

workedAuctions.label: Counter-example: a weak long suit starts with waiting, then the second negative

calls.rationale: Responder waits because the long suit is too weak to count as a positive.
Opener continues to describe shape naturally.
The cheapest new suit becomes the artificial second negative, not a natural club bid.
Opener repeats hearts so the partnership may now stop below game.
Responder passes when the second negative was genuine and there is no real club suit to reveal.

items.opponentAction: Double or overcall of the two clubs opening
items.ourAction: Treat the competitive auction as outside this fixture's teaching scope.
items.note: Larry gives methods for these auctions, but this module stays narrower and teaches only the uncontested structure.

items.recommendation: Bid two diamonds waiting whenever no immediate positive suit is available.
items.note: The waiting call is about the absence of a good suit, not a strict zero to seven HCP ceiling.

items.recommendation: Show two hearts, two spades, three clubs, or three diamonds only with eight-plus HCP and a good five-plus card suit.
items.note: Larry's positive suit needs real honors, not just length.

items.recommendation: After opener rebids two notrump, continue with the normal notrump methods.
items.note: This fixture stops owning the Stayman and transfer follow-ups at that point.

items.recommendation: After a second negative, expect opener to repeat the suit; only then does repeating that second-negative suit become natural.
items.note: Larry's cancellation pattern turns the earlier artificial call back into a real suit once opener has given responder room to pass.

relatedLinks.discriminator: Stayman owns the three-club ask once opener has clarified a balanced two notrump rebid.
Transfers over opener's two notrump rebid belong to the standard Jacoby-transfer module, not to strong-two clubs itself.
Other two-level openings are preemptive; this one starts a forcing auction built on power.

teaching.purpose: Open very strong hands with an artificial 2C bid, then use the waiting, positive-suit, and second-negative structures to describe shape.
teaching.whenToUse: Open 2C with a balanced 22+ count or an unbalanced powerhouse with near-game tricks. As responder, bid 2D waiting unless you have 8+ HCP and a good 5+ suit.
whenNotToUse: With a weak hand and a long suit — use a Weak Two instead
2C is always artificial here — never open 2C to show clubs
teaching.tradeoff: 2C loses its natural club meaning in exchange for a forcing opening that prevents being passed out with a monster hand.
teaching.principle: Start with the waiting 2D reply unless responder has real suit quality; opener then clarifies balanced versus natural-suit routes, and after a second negative repeats the suit so responder can pass or later show that suit naturally.
teaching.roles: Opener describes; responder evaluates. The auction is forcing except after 2C-2D-2NT and the second-negative partscore escapes, and the later no-trump continuations hand off to the normal NT modules.
